Chapter 1 High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 1.1 Overview 1.2 Learning Objectives 1.3 Course Design 1.4 Suggested Use

Chapter 1 High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 1.1 Overview This course introduces high-performance computing (HPC) using the Lonestar6 system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center (TACC). The course is designed as both: a textbook a lab manual a practical workflow guide We move from: basic system access to Linux fundamentals to SLURM job scheduling to compiling software to parallel computing to containers to a capstone project to a practical appendix 1.2 Learning Objectives By the end of the course, students should be able to: explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes navigate Linux confidently on a shared cluster manage files, permissions, and environment variables load software through modules submit and monitor SLURM batch jobs debug basic job failures compile C and C++ software for HPC systems understand the role of MPI, OpenMP, and containers in modern HPC workflows 1.3 Course Design Each chapter includes: overview mental model commands walkthrough common mistakes lab stretch goal 1.4 Suggested Use This material works well for: upper-level undergraduate instruction graduate onboarding research-computing workshops self-paced training for new cluster users

Chapter 2 Introduction to High-Performance Computing (HPC) 2.1 Overview 2.2 What is HPC? 2.3 Mental Model: The Cluster 2.4 Mental Model: Login Nodes vs Compute Nodes 2.5 Lonestar6 in Practice 2.6 Connecting to the System 2.7 First Prompt 2.8 Storage Areas 2.9 Basic Linux Commands 2.10 Editing Files 2.11 Transfer Files 2.12 First Small Script 2.13 Mental Model: What Just Happened? 2.14 Common Mistakes 2.15 Lab 1 — Getting Started on LS6 2.16 Stretch Goal 2.17 Looking Ahead 2.18 Key Takeaways

Chapter 2 Introduction to High-Performance Computing (HPC) 2.1 Overview High-performance computing (HPC) allows us to solve problems that are too large, too slow, or too complex for an ordinary desktop or laptop computer. In this course, we will use the Lonestar6 (LS6) system at TACC as our working environment. We will move from: basic system access to job submission to compiling and running software to modern workflows using containers 2.2 What is HPC? At a high level, HPC is about: More compute power More memory Parallel execution Instead of one machine, we use a cluster. 2.3 Mental Model: The Cluster Think of an HPC system as a collection of computers working together. 2.3.1 Key Components Node → a single computer in the cluster Core → a CPU processing unit within a node Memory (RAM) → typically shared by processes on a node Interconnect → the high-speed network connecting nodes 2.4 Mental Model: Login Nodes vs Compute Nodes This is one of the most important distinctions in HPC. 2.4.1 Login Nodes Used for: editing files compiling code loading modules moving data submitting jobs Not used for: heavy computation long-running analyses large training runs 2.4.2 Compute Nodes Used for: actual program execution scheduled workloads parallel jobs batch jobs interactive compute sessions ⚠️ Important Rule Serious computation should not be run on the login nodes. 2.5 Lonestar6 in Practice On Lonestar6, users typically connect with SSH, work in a Linux shell, manage files across storage areas, and submit workloads to SLURM. 2.6 Connecting to the System Use SSH to connect to LS6. ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu 2.7 First Prompt You may see something like: [username@login1 ~]$ This tells you: who you are which machine you are on your current directory 2.8 Storage Areas You will often work with several storage areas: $HOME → personal home directory $WORK → project-oriented work storage $SCRATCH → high-performance temporary storage 2.8.1 Check where you are pwd 2.8.2 List files ls 2.9 Basic Linux Commands 2.9.1 Move around cd my_directory cd .. cd ~ 2.9.2 Create a directory mkdir project1 2.9.3 Copy a file cp file1.txt file2.txt 2.9.4 Move or rename a file mv oldname.txt newname.txt 2.9.5 Remove a file rm file1.txt 2.10 Editing Files Two common editors are: nano myfile.txt and vim myfile.txt 2.11 Transfer Files From your local system: scp myfile.txt your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu:~ 2.12 First Small Script Create a file: nano hello.sh Add: echo "Hello from LS6" Make it executable: chmod +x hello.sh Run it: ./hello.sh 2.13 Mental Model: What Just Happened? You: created a script changed its permissions ran a small command safely on a login node That is appropriate because the task is tiny and brief. 2.14 Common Mistakes running large jobs directly on the login node forgetting chmod +x losing temporary files in scratch storage forgetting to check current location with pwd 2.15 Lab 1 — Getting Started on LS6 2.15.1 Part 1: Login Connect via SSH Confirm identity: whoami 2.15.2 Part 2: Filesystem Create a working directory: mkdir hpc_course cd hpc_course pwd 2.15.3 Part 3: File Creation Create a script: nano hello.sh Add: echo "Hello HPC" 2.15.4 Part 4: Run It chmod +x hello.sh ./hello.sh 2.16 Stretch Goal Create a second script that prints: your username current directory current date Hint: whoami pwd date 2.17 Looking Ahead In the next chapter, we will deepen Linux skills by focusing on: permissions environment variables modules debugging habits 2.18 Key Takeaways HPC systems use clusters Login nodes are for setup and lightweight work Compute nodes are for actual computation Linux command-line literacy is essential Storage choices matter

Chapter 3 Linux Fundamentals for HPC 3.1 Overview 3.2 Mental Model: You Are in a Shared Environment 3.3 The Linux Filesystem 3.4 Inspecting the System 3.5 File Permissions 3.6 Visual Breakdown 3.7 Changing Permissions 3.8 Why Permissions Matter in HPC 3.9 Environment Variables 3.10 Mental Model: PATH 3.11 Modules System 3.12 Basic Module Commands 3.13 Example: Using a Compiler 3.14 Finding Files 3.15 Viewing File Contents 3.16 Combining Commands with Pipes 3.17 Disk Usage 3.18 Processes 3.19 Optional R Integration 3.20 Common Mistakes 3.21 Debugging Mindset in Linux 3.22 Lab 2 — Linux and Environment Mastery 3.23 Stretch Goal 3.24 Transition to HPC Workflows 3.25 Looking Ahead 3.26 Key Takeaways

Chapter 3 Linux Fundamentals for HPC 3.1 Overview In this chapter, we develop the Linux skills needed to work effectively on an HPC system. You will learn: how files and permissions work how to inspect and manage your environment how software is exposed through modules how to think like a debugger on a shared system 3.2 Mental Model: You Are in a Shared Environment Unlike a personal laptop, an HPC system is: multi-user resource-managed environment-driven structured for reproducibility Every action you take should be: deliberate traceable repeatable 3.3 The Linux Filesystem Let’s visualize where you are: / ├── home/ │ └── your_username/ ├── work/ │ └── project_space/ ├── scratch/ │ └── temporary_files/ 3.3.1 Key Idea /home → safe, small /work → project data /scratch → fast, temporary 3.4 Inspecting the System 3.4.1 Where am I? pwd 3.4.2 What’s here? ls -l 3.4.3 What does -l show? -rw-r--r-- 1 user group size date filename 3.5 File Permissions 3.5.1 Mental Model Every file has: Owner Group Others Each has: read (r) write (w) execute (x) 3.6 Visual Breakdown -rwxr-xr-- │││││││││ │││││││└── others │││││└──── group ││└──────── owner 3.7 Changing Permissions 3.7.1 Make a file executable chmod +x script.sh 3.7.2 Remove write permission chmod -w file.txt 3.7.3 Numeric mode chmod 755 script.sh Meaning: user: rwx group: r-x others: r-x ⚠️ Common HPC Failure Many jobs fail because a script is missing execute permission. 3.8 Why Permissions Matter in HPC Common failure modes include: a script that will not run a file you cannot edit a shared file with the wrong access settings 3.9 Environment Variables 3.9.1 What are they? Variables that define your working environment. 3.9.2 View them env 3.9.3 Common examples echo $HOME echo $PATH 3.10 Mental Model: PATH PATH tells the system where to look for executables. echo $PATH If a program is not in PATH, Linux may not find it. 3.10.1 Add to PATH temporarily export PATH=$HOME/bin:$PATH 💡 Debugging Tip If you see command not found, check: echo $PATH which <command> 3.11 Modules System HPC systems do not expose all software globally. Instead, they use modules. 3.11.1 Mental Model Modules are loadable software environments. 3.12 Basic Module Commands 3.12.1 List available modules module avail 3.12.2 Load a module module load gcc 3.12.3 Check loaded modules module list 3.12.4 Unload a module module unload gcc 🧠 Key Insight On HPC systems, software access is often environment-based, not system-wide in the way many users expect on a laptop. 3.13 Example: Using a Compiler module load gcc which gcc gcc --version 3.14 Finding Files find . -name "file.txt" 3.15 Viewing File Contents cat file.txt less file.txt head file.txt tail file.txt 3.16 Combining Commands with Pipes cat file.txt | grep "error" 3.16.1 Pipe Mental Model Think: one command produces output, the next command filters or transforms it 3.17 Disk Usage 3.17.1 Check filesystem space df -h 3.17.2 Check directory sizes du -sh * 3.18 Processes 3.18.1 See your processes ps -u $USER 3.18.2 Kill a process kill <pid> 3.19 Optional R Integration You can inspect environment settings inside R. Sys.getenv("PATH") You can also run shell commands from R. system("echo $HOME") 3.20 Common Mistakes forgetting chmod +x PATH confusion not loading needed modules filling up $HOME losing temporary work in $SCRATCH 3.21 Debugging Mindset in Linux When something goes wrong, ask: 3.21.1 1. Where am I? pwd 3.21.2 2. What files are here? ls -l 3.21.3 3. Do I have permission? ls -l script.sh 3.21.4 4. Is the program available? which gcc 3.21.5 5. Is my environment correct? module list 3.22 Lab 2 — Linux and Environment Mastery 3.22.1 Part 1: Permissions Create a file: touch test.sh Check permissions: ls -l Make executable: chmod +x test.sh 3.22.2 Part 2: Environment Variables echo $HOME echo $PATH export COURSE=HPC echo $COURSE 3.22.3 Part 3: Modules module avail module load gcc which gcc gcc --version 3.22.4 Part 4: Disk Usage df -h du -sh ~ 3.22.5 Part 5: File Search touch example.txt find . -name "example.txt" 3.23 Stretch Goal Create a custom bin/ directory Add it to PATH Put a script inside it Run it without using the full path 3.24 Transition to HPC Workflows So far, you have been: navigating the system editing files running small commands But on an HPC system, real work is usually not done directly from the command line on the login node. Instead, you will: write a job script request resources submit the work to the scheduler 3.25 Looking Ahead In the next chapter, we introduce SLURM, the scheduler that controls computation on the system. 3.26 Key Takeaways Permissions control execution and modification PATH controls command discovery Modules manage software environments Disk and process awareness matter Reproducible HPC work depends on environment awareness

Chapter 4 SLURM: Job Scheduling on HPC Systems 4.1 Overview 4.2 Mental Model: Renting Compute Power 4.3 The Basic Workflow 4.4 Why a Scheduler Exists 4.5 What Is a Job? 4.6 Your First Job Script 4.7 Breaking Down the Script 4.8 Submitting a Job 4.9 Monitoring Jobs 4.10 Common Job States 4.11 Output Files 4.12 Mental Model: What Just Happened? 4.13 Important Distinction: Login Node vs Compute Node 4.14 Adding a Delay to Observe the Queue 4.15 Common Mistakes 4.16 Inspecting Completed Jobs 4.17 Cancelling a Job 4.18 Minimal Serial Job Example 4.19 Example with Module Loading 4.20 Example with Multiple Tasks 4.21 Mental Model: Resources 4.22 Lab 3 — First SLURM Jobs 4.23 Debugging Checklist 4.24 Failure-Based Exercise 4.25 Best Practices 4.26 Conceptual Bridge 4.27 Looking Ahead 4.28 Key Takeaways

Chapter 4 SLURM: Job Scheduling on HPC Systems 4.1 Overview On an HPC system, you do not usually run serious computational work directly on the command line. Instead, you submit jobs to a scheduler. On Lonestar6, that scheduler is SLURM. This chapter introduces: what a scheduler does how to write a SLURM script how to submit and monitor jobs how to debug common problems 4.2 Mental Model: Renting Compute Power Think of SLURM as a resource manager. You request: nodes tasks time sometimes memory or other constraints Then the system decides when and where your job runs. 4.3 The Basic Workflow User → Job Script → SLURM → Compute Node → Output File 4.4 Why a Scheduler Exists Without a scheduler: users would compete directly for resources jobs would collide the cluster would become disorganized With a scheduler: resources are allocated fairly jobs are queued usage is tracked 4.5 What Is a Job? A job is a request that describes: what program to run how many resources it needs how long it may run what environment it needs 4.6 Your First Job Script Create a file: nano job.slurm Add this content: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Hello from compute node" hostname date 4.7 Breaking Down the Script 4.7.1 Shebang #!/bin/bash Tells the system to use Bash. 4.7.2 Job name #SBATCH -J hello_job This gives your job a readable name. 4.7.3 Number of nodes #SBATCH -N 1 Request one node. 4.7.4 Number of tasks #SBATCH -n 1 Request one task. 4.7.5 Wall time #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 Maximum runtime of one minute. 4.7.6 Partition #SBATCH -p normal Request the normal queue or partition. 4.8 Submitting a Job sbatch job.slurm You should see a message similar to: Submitted batch job 123456 4.9 Monitoring Jobs 4.9.1 Show your queued and running jobs squeue -u $USER This shows: job ID partition job name state elapsed time nodes used 4.10 Common Job States PD → pending R → running CG → completing CD → completed 4.11 Output Files When the job finishes, you will usually see: slurm-123456.out View it with: cat slurm-123456.out Or more generally: cat slurm-*.out 4.12 Mental Model: What Just Happened? You: wrote a batch script submitted it with sbatch SLURM placed it in the queue the scheduler assigned resources the script ran on a compute node output was written to a file 4.13 Important Distinction: Login Node vs Compute Node When you type hostname on the login node, you will see a login-node name. When the same command runs inside a SLURM job, it shows the compute node that actually executed your work. 4.14 Adding a Delay to Observe the Queue Edit your script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Starting job" hostname sleep 30 echo "Finishing job" Submit it: sbatch job.slurm Then monitor: squeue -u $USER 4.15 Common Mistakes 4.15.1 1. Job never starts Possible reasons: requested too many resources requested the wrong partition requested too much wall time 4.15.2 2. Job fails immediately Possible reasons: script syntax error bad command missing module missing input file 4.15.3 3. No output appears Possible reasons: job is still queued wrong working directory output redirected elsewhere ⚠️ Very Common Beginner Error Students often assume sbatch runs the job immediately. It does not. It submits the job to the scheduler. 4.16 Inspecting Completed Jobs A useful accounting command is: sacct A more targeted version is: sacct -j <jobid> 4.17 Cancelling a Job If you need to stop a job: scancel <jobid> 4.18 Minimal Serial Job Example #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J serial_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Running serial test" python myscript.py 4.19 Example with Module Loading #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J gcc_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal module load gcc echo "Compiler path:" which gcc gcc --version 4.20 Example with Multiple Tasks #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J multitask_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Testing multiple tasks" hostname 4.21 Mental Model: Resources A good beginner mental model is: A SLURM script is a contract: “Here is what I want to run, and here are the resources I need.” 4.22 Lab 3 — First SLURM Jobs 4.22.1 Part 1: Your first job Create job.slurm Add the minimal script Submit with: sbatch job.slurm Monitor with: squeue -u $USER View output file 4.22.2 Part 2: Observe a running job Modify the script to include: sleep 30 Resubmit and monitor the job while it runs. 4.22.3 Part 3: Change resource request Change: #SBATCH -n 1 to: #SBATCH -n 4 Resubmit and compare behavior. 4.22.4 Part 4: Load a module in the job Modify the script to include: module load gcc which gcc gcc --version Submit and inspect output. 4.23 Debugging Checklist When a job fails, ask: 4.23.1 1. Did it submit? squeue -u $USER 4.23.2 2. Did it complete or fail? sacct 4.23.3 3. What does the output file say? cat slurm-*.out 4.23.4 4. Did I load the right modules? Check the script itself and your environment assumptions. 4.23.5 5. Am I in the correct directory? pwd ls 4.24 Failure-Based Exercise Try intentionally breaking a script in one of these ways: misspell a command load a module that is unavailable request an unrealistic resource combination refer to a missing file Then use: sacct output files script inspection to diagnose what happened. 4.25 Best Practices keep scripts small and readable request realistic wall time name jobs clearly inspect output every time use version-controlled job scripts when possible 4.26 Conceptual Bridge Up to this point, students have learned: how to access the system how to work in Linux how to manage their environment how to submit jobs The next step is to understand how software is actually built and prepared for these environments. 4.27 Looking Ahead In the next chapter, we will move into compiling software: using gcc and g++ understanding build steps working with modules and toolchains preparing programs for HPC execution 4.28 Key Takeaways Serious computation is submitted through SLURM A job script describes both commands and resources sbatch submits work squeue monitors queued and running jobs sacct helps inspect job history Output files are central to debugging

Chapter 5 Compiling C and C++ on HPC Systems 5.1 Overview 5.2 Mental Model: From Source Code to Executable 5.3 Why Compilation Matters in HPC 5.4 Compilers: gcc vs g++ 5.5 Loading a Compiler (Modules) 5.6 Your First C Program 5.7 Compile the Program 5.8 Run the Program 5.9 Mental Model: What Just Happened? 5.10 File Inspection 5.11 C++ Example 5.12 Compilation Flags 5.13 Common Optimization Levels 5.14 Multiple Source Files 5.15 Header Files 5.16 Introduction to Makefiles 5.17 Mental Model: Makefiles 5.18 Modules and Compilation 5.19 Where to Compile 5.20 Running Compiled Code with SLURM 5.21 Common Mistakes 5.22 Debugging Compilation Errors 5.23 Lab 4 — Compiling on HPC 5.24 Stretch Goal 5.25 Transition to Parallel Computing 5.26 Key Takeaways

Chapter 5 Compiling C and C++ on HPC Systems 5.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from running existing programs to building software from source. You will learn: what compilation is and why it matters how to use gcc and g++ how to build programs on HPC systems how modules affect compilers and libraries how to prepare compiled programs for SLURM execution 5.2 Mental Model: From Source Code to Executable A compiled workflow looks like this: source code (.c / .cpp) ↓ compiler (gcc / g++) ↓ executable (binary file) ↓ run on compute node 5.3 Why Compilation Matters in HPC On HPC systems: software is often not pre-installed you may need specific compiler versions performance depends on how code is compiled 🧠 Key Insight Compilation is not just “building code” — it is configuring how your program interacts with the hardware. 5.4 Compilers: gcc vs g++ gcc → C programs g++ → C++ programs 5.5 Loading a Compiler (Modules) Before compiling, you must load a compiler. module load gcc Check it: which gcc gcc --version ⚠️ Common Error Forgetting to load a compiler module will often result in: command not found 5.6 Your First C Program Create a file: nano hello.c Add: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello from C on HPC\n"); return 0; } 5.7 Compile the Program gcc hello.c -o hello 5.8 Run the Program ./hello 5.9 Mental Model: What Just Happened? hello.c → source file gcc → compiler -o hello → output executable 5.10 File Inspection Check what was created: ls -l You should see: hello.c hello (executable) 5.11 C++ Example Create a file: nano hello.cpp Add: #include <iostream> int main() { std::cout << "Hello from C++ on HPC" << std::endl; return 0; } Compile: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp Run: ./hello_cpp 5.12 Compilation Flags Basic flags: gcc hello.c -o hello -O2 -O2 → optimization level 5.13 Common Optimization Levels -O0 → no optimization -O2 → standard optimization -O3 → aggressive optimization 💡 Tip Use -O2 for most HPC workflows unless you know you need something else. 5.14 Multiple Source Files Example structure: main.c helper.c helper.h Compile: gcc main.c helper.c -o program 5.15 Header Files Headers (.h) define interfaces. Example: int add(int a, int b); 5.16 Introduction to Makefiles For larger projects, use make. Create a file: nano Makefile Add: all: \tgcc hello.c -o hello Run: make 5.17 Mental Model: Makefiles Think: “Makefiles automate compilation steps” 5.18 Modules and Compilation Different modules provide: different compilers different library versions different performance characteristics Example: module load gcc module load openmpi 5.19 Where to Compile Compile on: ✅ login nodes (small builds) ❌ compute nodes (unless needed for large builds) 5.20 Running Compiled Code with SLURM Create a job script: nano run.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J compile_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal ./hello Submit: sbatch run.slurm 5.21 Common Mistakes forgetting module load gcc missing -o flag running in wrong directory not including all source files 5.22 Debugging Compilation Errors If compilation fails, check compiler messages carefully. Common issues include: syntax errors missing headers undefined references incorrect include paths 5.23 Lab 4 — Compiling on HPC 5.23.1 Part 1: Basic Compilation Create hello.c Compile: gcc hello.c -o hello Run: ./hello 5.23.2 Part 2: C++ Program Create hello.cpp Compile: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp Run: ./hello_cpp 5.23.3 Part 3: Optimization Compile with optimization: gcc hello.c -o hello_opt -O2 5.23.4 Part 4: Run with SLURM Create run.slurm Submit: sbatch run.slurm Inspect output 5.24 Stretch Goal Create a program with multiple source files Write a Makefile Compile using make Run through SLURM 5.25 Transition to Parallel Computing So far, you have: accessed the system used Linux effectively managed your environment submitted jobs compiled programs Next, we will scale up: running programs across multiple cores using MPI and OpenMP understanding parallel performance 5.26 Key Takeaways Compilation transforms source code into executables gcc and g++ are core tools Modules control compiler environments Optimization affects performance Compiled programs are typically run through SLURM

Chapter 6 Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP 6.1 Overview 6.2 Mental Model: Why Parallel Computing? 6.3 Visual: Serial vs Parallel 6.4 Two Main Models 6.5 Mental Model: Processes vs Threads 6.6 Loading MPI 6.7 MPI Hello World (C) 6.8 Compile MPI Program 6.9 Run Locally for Testing 6.10 MPI Job Script 6.11 Expected Output 6.12 OpenMP Example 6.13 Compile OpenMP Program 6.14 Run OpenMP 6.15 OpenMP SLURM Script 6.16 Hybrid Concept 6.17 Mental Model 6.18 Performance Thinking 6.19 Common Mistakes 6.20 Debugging Checklist 6.21 Lab 5 — Parallel Computing 6.22 Stretch Goal 6.23 Transition 6.24 Key Takeaways

Chapter 6 Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP 6.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from single-core programs to parallel computing. You will learn: the difference between serial and parallel execution the difference between processes and threads how MPI and OpenMP work how to run parallel jobs with SLURM how to think about performance and scaling 6.2 Mental Model: Why Parallel Computing? A single core can only do one thing at a time. Parallel computing allows you to: split a problem into pieces solve those pieces simultaneously combine the results 6.3 Visual: Serial vs Parallel Serial: Task1 → Task2 → Task3 → Task4 Parallel: Task1 Task2 → run at the same time Task3 Task4 6.4 Two Main Models 6.4.1 MPI (Message Passing Interface) multiple processes can run across multiple nodes processes communicate via messages 6.4.2 OpenMP multiple threads usually within one node shared memory model 6.5 Mental Model: Processes vs Threads MPI: Process 1 → separate memory Process 2 → separate memory OpenMP: Thread 1 → shared memory Thread 2 → shared memory 🧠 Key Insight MPI is for distributed computing across nodes. OpenMP is for shared memory within a node. 6.6 Loading MPI module load openmpi 6.7 MPI Hello World (C) Create a file: nano hello_mpi.c Add: #include <mpi.h> #include <stdio.h> int main(int argc, char** argv) { MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); int rank, size; MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size); MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } 6.8 Compile MPI Program mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi 6.9 Run Locally for Testing mpirun -np 4 ./hello_mpi ⚠️ Important On HPC systems, production runs should go through SLURM. 6.10 MPI Job Script Create: nano mpi.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J mpi_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi Submit: sbatch mpi.slurm 6.11 Expected Output Hello from process 0 of 4 Hello from process 1 of 4 Hello from process 2 of 4 Hello from process 3 of 4 6.12 OpenMP Example Create: nano hello_omp.c Add: #include <stdio.h> #include <omp.h> int main() { #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); printf("Hello from thread %d\n", id); } return 0; } 6.13 Compile OpenMP Program gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp 6.14 Run OpenMP export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp 6.15 OpenMP SLURM Script Create: nano omp.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J omp_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Submit: sbatch omp.slurm 6.16 Hybrid Concept You can combine MPI and OpenMP: MPI across nodes OpenMP within nodes 6.17 Mental Model Node 1: Process 1 → Threads Process 2 → Threads Node 2: Process 3 → Threads Process 4 → Threads 6.18 Performance Thinking We care about how runtime changes as cores increase. 6.18.1 Strong Scaling Same problem size, more cores. 6.18.2 Weak Scaling Problem size increases with cores. 6.18.3 Simple Timing Example time ./hello 💡 Insight More cores do not always mean faster results. Communication overhead matters. 6.19 Common Mistakes using MPI without loading the module mismatch between -n and program expectations forgetting OMP_NUM_THREADS running MPI directly on the login node misunderstanding cores vs tasks 6.20 Debugging Checklist 6.20.1 Check job: squeue -u $USER 6.20.2 Check output: cat slurm-*.out 6.20.3 Check modules: module list 6.20.4 Check resource request: nodes (-N) tasks (-n) cores (-c) 6.21 Lab 5 — Parallel Computing 6.21.1 Part 1: MPI Create hello_mpi.c Compile: mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi Create SLURM script Run with 4 tasks 6.21.2 Part 2: OpenMP Create hello_omp.c Compile: gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp Run with: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 6.21.3 Part 3: Compare run serial version run MPI version run OpenMP version 6.21.4 Part 4: Scaling Experiment Run MPI with different task counts and compare behavior and runtime. 6.22 Stretch Goal modify program to do actual computation measure runtime differences plot speedup 6.23 Transition You now understand: how to compile code how to run jobs how to use multiple cores Next, we modernize workflows with containers and reproducibility. 6.24 Key Takeaways Parallel computing splits work across cores MPI uses processes across nodes OpenMP uses threads within a node SLURM controls resource allocation Performance depends on scaling and overhead

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC 7.1 Overview 7.2 Mental Model: What is a Container? 7.3 Visual: Container Concept 7.4 Why Containers Matter in HPC 7.5 Docker vs Apptainer 7.6 Mental Model 7.7 Checking for Apptainer 7.8 Pulling a Container 7.9 Running a Container 7.10 Interactive Shell 7.11 Mental Model: What Just Happened? 7.12 Running Python in a Container 7.13 Binding Directories 7.14 SLURM + Containers 7.15 Running Applications in Containers 7.16 Comparing Workflows 7.17 Reproducibility 7.18 Common Mistakes 7.19 Storage Considerations 7.20 Debugging Containers 7.21 Lab 6 — Containers on HPC 7.22 Stretch Goal 7.23 Transition to Capstone 7.24 Key Takeaways

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC 7.1 Overview In this chapter, we introduce containers as a tool for reproducibility and portability in HPC. You will learn: what containers are and why they matter the difference between Docker and HPC containers how Apptainer works how to run containers on HPC systems how to integrate containers with SLURM 7.2 Mental Model: What is a Container? A container packages: your program all required libraries system dependencies environment configuration 7.3 Visual: Container Concept Your Program + Libraries + Dependencies + Environment ----------------- Container Image 7.4 Why Containers Matter in HPC Without containers: dependency conflicts occur software versions differ between systems workflows are hard to reproduce With containers: environments are consistent workflows are portable results are reproducible 🧠 Key Insight Containers solve the “it works on my machine” problem. 7.5 Docker vs Apptainer 7.5.1 Docker widely used in industry typically requires privileges not appropriate on shared HPC systems better suited to development and deployment workflows outside the cluster 7.5.2 Apptainer designed for HPC runs without root access in normal use integrates with SLURM commonly available on clusters 7.6 Mental Model Docker → development environment Apptainer → HPC execution environment 7.7 Checking for Apptainer which apptainer or: module load apptainer 7.8 Pulling a Container apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu 7.9 Running a Container apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello from container" 7.10 Interactive Shell apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.11 Mental Model: What Just Happened? you downloaded a container image you executed a command inside it the container used the host kernel 7.12 Running Python in a Container apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 --version 7.13 Binding Directories apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls 💡 Tip Always bind your working directory when running containerized jobs. 7.14 SLURM + Containers Create: nano container.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal module load apptainer apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Running inside container" Submit: sbatch container.slurm Inspect output: cat slurm-*.out 7.15 Running Applications in Containers Example: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python script.py 7.16 Comparing Workflows 7.16.1 Without container load modules install dependencies manage versions 7.16.2 With container run container everything is included in the image or explicitly bound 7.17 Reproducibility Containers allow you to: rerun experiments later share environments with others ensure consistency across systems 7.18 Common Mistakes forgetting to load the Apptainer module not binding directories assuming the container has every needed tool installed using Docker commands directly on the cluster storing large images in $HOME 7.19 Storage Considerations Store containers in: $WORK $SCRATCH Avoid: $HOME for large image files 7.20 Debugging Containers 7.20.1 Check image exists ls *.sif 7.20.2 Test interactively apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.20.3 Check a command apptainer exec ubuntu.sif which python3 7.21 Lab 6 — Containers on HPC 7.21.1 Part 1: Pull Image apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu 7.21.2 Part 2: Run Command apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello container" 7.21.3 Part 3: Interactive Mode apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.21.4 Part 4: SLURM Job Create container.slurm and run the container through SLURM. 7.21.5 Part 5: Bind Directory apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls 7.22 Stretch Goal run a Python script inside a container compare container vs native environment document differences 7.23 Transition to Capstone You now understand: system access Linux fundamentals job scheduling compiling software parallel computing container workflows 7.24 Key Takeaways Containers package complete environments Apptainer is designed for HPC systems Containers improve reproducibility SLURM can run containerized jobs Containers simplify complex workflows

Chapter 8 Capstone Project: End-to-End HPC Workflow 8.1 Overview 8.2 Project Philosophy 8.3 General Requirements 8.4 Project Options 8.5 Option 1: Compile and Run 8.6 Option 2: Parallel Computing 8.7 Option 3: Containerized Workflow 8.8 Option 4: Hybrid Workflow (Advanced) 8.9 Deliverables 8.10 Suggested Directory Structure 8.11 Reproducibility Requirements 8.12 Resource Awareness 8.13 Debugging Reflection 8.14 Grading Rubric 8.15 Example Workflow Summary 8.16 Common Mistakes 8.17 Stretch Goals 8.18 Final Reflection 8.19 Key Takeaways

Chapter 8 Capstone Project: End-to-End HPC Workflow 8.1 Overview The capstone project is the final component of this course. In this project, you will demonstrate your ability to: work effectively in a Linux-based HPC environment manage software environments using modules and/or containers compile and run programs submit and monitor SLURM jobs use parallel computing where appropriate document and reproduce your workflow 8.2 Project Philosophy This project is not just about “getting something to run.” It is about: reproducibility clarity resource awareness debugging ability 🧠 Key Insight A successful HPC user is not just someone who runs code, but someone who can reproduce and explain their workflow. 8.3 General Requirements All projects must include: a working directory structure source code or scripts at least one SLURM job script output files from completed jobs documentation describing the workflow 8.4 Project Options You may choose one of the following project types. 8.5 Option 1: Compile and Run 8.5.1 Description Compile a C or C++ program and run it on the HPC system. 8.5.2 Requirements write or obtain a C/C++ program compile using gcc or g++ create a SLURM job script run the program through SLURM capture and analyze output 8.5.3 Suggested Extensions use multiple source files create a Makefile compare optimized vs non-optimized builds 8.6 Option 2: Parallel Computing 8.6.1 Description Run a parallel program using MPI or OpenMP. 8.6.2 Requirements compile an MPI or OpenMP program run with multiple cores use SLURM to request resources demonstrate scaling behavior 8.6.3 Suggested Extensions compare serial vs parallel performance run with different core counts interpret speedup results 8.7 Option 3: Containerized Workflow 8.7.1 Description Run an application inside a container on the HPC system. 8.7.2 Requirements pull or use a container image execute commands using Apptainer run a containerized job via SLURM demonstrate reproducibility 8.7.3 Suggested Extensions bind directories correctly compare container vs native environment document dependencies 8.8 Option 4: Hybrid Workflow (Advanced) 8.8.1 Description Combine multiple techniques: compiled code parallel execution containerization 8.8.2 Requirements compile a program run it in parallel with MPI or OpenMP optionally execute within a container submit through SLURM 8.9 Deliverables Your submission must include: 8.9.1 1. Code and Scripts source files (.c, .cpp, .py, etc.) SLURM job scripts (.slurm) optional Makefile 8.9.2 2. Output Files SLURM output files (slurm-*.out) any generated results 8.9.3 3. Documentation A short report that explains: what you built how you compiled it how you ran it what resources you requested what output you observed what problems you encountered how you debugged them 💡 Tip Include command snippets in your report to make your workflow reproducible. 8.10 Suggested Directory Structure project/ ├── src/ ├── scripts/ ├── jobs/ ├── output/ └── README.md 8.11 Reproducibility Requirements Your project should be runnable by another user. This means: all steps are documented required modules are listed scripts are complete paths are clear ⚠️ Common Failure “It works on my account” is not sufficient. 8.12 Resource Awareness You should demonstrate understanding of: number of cores requested wall time memory, if relevant 8.13 Debugging Reflection You must include at least one example of: a failure you encountered how you diagnosed it how you fixed it 8.14 Grading Rubric 8.14.1 Correctness (30%) program runs successfully output is produced SLURM job completes 8.14.2 Workflow Clarity (25%) steps are documented commands are clear structure is organized 8.14.3 Reproducibility (20%) another user could repeat your process environment is clearly defined 8.14.4 Resource Usage (15%) appropriate use of cores and time understanding of SLURM directives 8.14.5 Reflection (10%) discussion of challenges debugging explanation 8.15 Example Workflow Summary A strong project might include: Write a C program Compile with gcc -O2 Create a SLURM script Run the job Observe output Modify for parallel execution Compare performance Document the workflow 8.16 Common Mistakes missing SLURM script no documentation unclear directory structure hardcoded paths no debugging explanation incorrect resource requests 8.17 Stretch Goals For advanced students: run multi-node MPI jobs build software with dependencies integrate containers with parallel jobs measure and plot performance 8.18 Final Reflection At this point, you have learned to: navigate an HPC system manage Linux environments submit and monitor jobs compile and run software use parallel computing work with containers 8.19 Key Takeaways HPC workflows involve multiple layers reproducibility is critical SLURM is central to execution environment management matters debugging is part of the process

Chapter 9 Appendix: HPC Quick Reference 9.1 Overview 9.2 SSH 9.3 Basic Linux 9.4 Permissions 9.5 Environment and Modules 9.6 SLURM 9.7 Compilation 9.8 MPI 9.9 OpenMP 9.10 Containers

Chapter 9 Appendix: HPC Quick Reference 9.1 Overview This appendix provides a compact quick reference for common commands and workflow patterns used throughout the course. 9.2 SSH ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu 9.3 Basic Linux pwd ls -l cd mkdir project cp file1 file2 mv old new rm file 9.4 Permissions chmod +x script.sh chmod 755 script.sh 9.5 Environment and Modules echo $PATH module avail module load gcc module list module unload gcc 9.6 SLURM sbatch job.slurm squeue -u $USER sacct scancel <jobid> 9.7 Compilation gcc program.c -o program -O2 g++ program.cpp -o program make 9.8 MPI module load openmpi mpicc program.c -o program 9.9 OpenMP gcc -fopenmp program.c -o program export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 9.10 Containers module load apptainer apptainer exec image.sif command ```md ## Final Notes This appendix provides a quick reference for common HPC workflows. For full explanations and examples, refer back to the main chapters.
